title: Polymer clay resist texture technique
subtitle: Masking tape resist to create two-tone geometric patterning
excerpt: Strips of masking tape act as a resist on a conditioned polymer clay sheet. A contrasting colour is scraped across the exposed surface; when the tape is peeled away, the protected areas reveal the base colour in clean-edged lines or geometric shapes.
sourceNotes: Masking-tape resist on polymer clay technique derived from first principles.

glossaryTerms.definition: A PVC-based modelling material that cures in a domestic oven at 110-130°C.
Kneading polymer clay thoroughly before use to warm and plasticise it, making it smooth and free of air pockets.

content.text: The masking-tape resist technique creates clean geometric patterns on
polymer clay

by using tape to protect areas of the base colour while a contrasting colour is scraped across the rest. When the tape is removed, the covered areas appear as crisp lines against the contrast colour. The resulting panel can be cut into pendants, brooch blanks, or earring components.

attrs.content: Use a dedicated oven tray. Do not share with food-prep cookware.

content.text: Preparing the clay
Thorough
conditioning

of both colours is essential. Under-conditioned clay tears rather than accepting the scraping step cleanly. Roll the base colour to 3 mm thick and the contrast colour to 2 mm thick. Both sheets should be roughly the same area, approximately 80 mm x 80 mm.
Applying the resist

Press strips of masking tape firmly onto the base sheet. Vary the angle and spacing to build the pattern: parallel strips give stripes, crossed strips give a grid, diagonal strips give chevrons. Press down firmly along each tape edge with a fingernail so the contrast colour cannot bleed underneath.
Adding the contrast layer
Cut thin slices (1 mm) from the contrast clay sheet and scatter them across the taped base sheet, covering both the tape and the exposed base clay.
Draw the metal rib or card firmly across the surface in a single stroke, spreading the contrast clay into a thin, even layer.
The tape strips will stand slightly proud of the scraped surface. This is correct.
Peeling the resist

Peel each tape strip away in one smooth pull, lifting at a low angle to the surface. The contrast clay on top of the tape lifts away with it, exposing the base colour beneath in a clean-edged line. If any contrast clay bridges the tape edge, score along the tape line with the fettling knife tip before peeling.
Curing and using the panel

Cut the finished panel to shape with the fettling knife. Cure on a dedicated tray at 130°C for 20-25 minutes depending on thickness. Apply the technique before cutting to shape, or cut the shape first and apply the resist to the blank.
Variations
Use three colours in sequence: apply tape, add second colour, cure lightly, peel, then add a third colour layer.
Use torn paper strips as the resist to get ragged, organic-edged lines rather than geometric ones.

items.symptom: Contrast clay bleeds under tape edges
items.cause: Tape edges not pressed down firmly before adding contrast clay
items.fix: After placing tape, press each edge firmly with a fingernail, then re-press after adding contrast clay before scraping

items.symptom: Contrast clay tears and drags rather than scraping flat
items.cause: Clay not conditioned enough; too stiff and crumbly to spread
items.fix: Condition both colours more thoroughly. Warm clay in your hands for two minutes before rolling
